metadata.file_name: 85. Ludwig et al. (2024). Predicting problem-solving success in an office simulation applying N-grams and a random forest to behavioral process data.pdf
metadata.title: Predicting problem-solving success in an office simulation applying N-grams and a random forest to behavioral process data

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses a single-sample, in-person dataset of 234 VET students who completed a browser-based office simulation (Luca). The manuscript describes preprocessing of log files (JSON export, cleaning/aggregation, early-window extraction) and then trains random-forest classifiers using train/test splits and 10-fold cross-validation for AUC. No complex survey design, sampling weights, or replicate weights (e.g., BRR/jackknife) are mentioned, and the analysis is framed as voluntary participation in a controlled study setting rather than a population-representative ILSA-style survey. Because the paper applies standard ML classifiers (random forest) without any stated weighting procedure, the analysis is treated as unweighted.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The dependent variable is a binary classification of problem-solving success derived from fine-grained rubric sum scores with a cut score (and exclusion of borderline cases), not a cognitive achievement score generated via plausible values. The paper does not describe any missing-data strategy for the log-derived features (e.g., how missing/empty early-window sequences were handled), which is a reporting gap for transparency. However, the PV framework is not applicable because the outcome is rubric-based classification rather than PV-based achievement.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Analytic sample consists of 234 VET students who worked on a supplier-selection scenario in the web-based office simulation Luca. Participants were excluded if they worked outside the web-based simulation (removed during log preprocessing). For the success classification, rubric sum scores were dichotomized using a threshold of 53 points, and borderline cases (n=10) were excluded to create two classes of equal size for balanced classification.

main_findings.dataset_used: Luca office simulation (VET supplier selection scenario) — early process windows (first 5/10/20 min)
main_findings.target_variable: Problem-solving success (binary class; more vs less successful)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Random Forest (M1 <5 min, M2 <10 min, M3 <20 min): Accuracy 0.53/0.60/0.60; AUC ROC 0.61/0.63/0.67; F1 (less successful) 0.53/0.64/0.63; F1 (more successful) 0.53/0.56/0.57. With feature selection (robust models): AUC improved from 0.61→0.65 (M1robust), 0.63→0.66 (M2robust), and ~0.70 (M3robust).
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using Luca office-simulation log data from VET students, the study leveraged early-window process indicators captured via n-gram/bigram behavior patterns—especially note-taking, relevant document access, spreadsheet calculation, and attachment-download sequences—to predict binary problem-solving success, finding that predictive performance improves as the early window expands from 5 to 20 minutes (AUC ROC 0.61→0.67) and further improves with feature selection (up to ~0.70). The model is a predictive classifier (not causal), and the paper does not report a missing-data strategy for log-derived features. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: The study analyzes log data from 234 VET students who completed a supplier-selection scenario in the browser-based office simulation Luca. Using a random-forest classifier trained on n-gram/bigram features from early processing windows (first 5, 10, and 20 minutes), the authors predict a binary problem-solving success label derived from rubric scores (borderline cases excluded). Performance is weak when predicting from the first 5 minutes (Accuracy=0.53; AUC ROC=0.61; F1≈0.53 for both classes) but improves for larger early windows (Accuracy=0.60 for both <10 and <20 min; AUC ROC=0.63 and 0.67, respectively). Feature selection using robust bigrams increases AUC further (up to ~0.70 for the <20 min window). The analysis is unweighted (no survey weights described) and the paper does not document a missing-data handling procedure for the log-derived predictors.
